text: The surgeon identifies the duodenum and ileocolic vessels, sealing the ileocolic pedicle with a radio frequency device. The dissection continues with the duodenum and pancreas, sealing the right branch of the middle colic vessels, followed by a medial to lateral dissection of the right retrocolic space along Toll's fascia.
The surgeon performs a bottom-to-top dissection over the right colic flexure to complete the cleavage of the right colon from the right peritoneal lining, taking care to avoid injury to the ureter.

Following specimen resection, the surgeon performs a primary anastomosis using a stapler to create a side-to-side isoperistaltic connection between the distal ileum and the transverse colon. The procedure concludes with a laparoscopic hand-sewn double-layer closure of the enterotomy.

The surgeon etches the peritoneal leaf of the sigmoid and proximal rectum from a cranial to caudal direction. The inferior mesenteric vein is sectioned at its root, followed by the closure and section of the inferior mesenteric artery while preserving the left colic artery due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the left mesocolon insertion and begins cleaving the mesorectal fascia from the precircular fascia along the holy plane. The top-bottom dislodging technique is used to avoid entering the precircular fascia.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection between the perirectal visceral fascia and the lateral perietal fascia of the pelvis to avoid ureter lesion, completing the left colon cleavage from the left perietal colic. On the right side, the peritoneum incision at the base of the mesosigma is extended downward and forward towards the rectal pocket, with the sigma retracted to the left.
The surgeon completes the rectal dissection by incising the peritoneal reflection, which frees the anterior rectal wall from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon extracts the specimens through a four-centimeter suprapubic mini laparotomy. The rectum is perforated along the suture line, and the stapler head is secured, ensuring the colon remains correctly oriented on the vascular axis. The anastomosis is performed using a technique similar to open surgery.
